evidence.quote: We are thrilled to announce Gemma 4.
For the first time ever, we're releasing Gemma under an open-source Apache 2.0 license.
Gemma 4 is a family of open models designed to run directly on the hardware you own: phone, laptops, and desktop.

Gemma 4 is built for the agentic era. It can handle complex logic, multi-step planning, and agentic workflows... It features native support for tool use, allowing you to build agents that plan and act on your behalf.

videos.summary: Google DeepMind announces Gemma 4, a new family of open-source models released under an Apache 2.0 license. These models are designed for local inference on various hardware, support agentic workflows with tool use, and offer multimodal capabilities. The release includes 26B MoE and 31B dense models for personal computers, and efficient 2B and 4B models for mobile and IoT devices, all while emphasizing enterprise-grade security.
videos.title: What’s new in Gemma 4?
videos.transcript_text: We are thrilled to announce Gemma [music] 4. Built from the same world-class research and technology behind Gemini 3, Gemma 4 is a family of open models designed to run directly on the hardware you own: phone, laptops, [music] and desktop. For the first time ever, we're releasing Gemma under an open-source Apache 2.0 license. Gemma 4 is built for the agentic era. It can handle complex logic, multi-step [music] planning, and agentic workflows, making optimal use of tokens for its intelligence. The bigger model perform well with a context [music] window of up to 1/4 million tokens, allowing you to analyze entire code bases, and all multi-turn agentic use cases. It features native support for tool use, allowing you to build agents that plan and act on [music] your behalf. Let's break down the model family now. First, a 26B mixture of experts [music] and a 31B dense models. These provide frontier intelligence directly on your personal computer. You can run state-of-the-art local reasoning and coding pipeline without needing to upload data outside of your control environment. The 26B MoE with 3.8 billion activated parameters is exceptionally fast, while 31B is optimized for output quality. Then, we have our effective 2B and effective 4B models. Engineered for maximum memory efficiency, these models bring a whole new level of intelligence to mobile and IoT devices. [music] With combined audio and vision support for real-time processing, they can see and hear the world. All of this while natively supporting over 140 languages. Now, [music] let's test our effective 2B on multilingual and agentic tasks. Hey, Gemma. Est-ce que tu peux trouver un restaurant français à San ? Please reply to me in English. Amazing, we have a winner. As open models become more central to enterprise infrastructure, security is paramount. Developed by Google DeepMind, Gemma 4 undergoes the same rigorous security protocols >> [music] >> as our proprietary models, giving enterprise and developer a trusted foundation to build on top of. We want you to be able to use Gemma 4 with the tools you already know and love. You can download the weights and start experimenting today. [music] We cannot wait to see what you create next.

videos.summary: OpenAI introduces two new real-time audio models in its API: GPT Realtime Translate, capable of live translation across 70 languages, and GPT Realtime 2, designed for intelligent voice agents that can reason, perform parallel tool calling, and integrate with various systems like CRMs and dashboards. The models aim to enable natural, continuous conversations and break down language barriers, making voice a primary interface.

videos.summary: The video introduces DeepSeek V4, a new open-weight AI model detailed in a 58-page research paper. It boasts a 1 million token context window and its Pro version reportedly matches or exceeds the performance of frontier models like Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro, particularly in recall and coding tasks, while being significantly more cost-effective. The model achieves this through innovative KV-cache compression techniques, including token-level compression, Heavily Compressed Attention, and Compressed Sparse Attention, which reduce memory needs by 90%. videos.summary: The video provides an in-depth analysis of the newly released GPT 5.5 and DeepSeek V4 models, comparing their performance against competitors like Anthropic's Opus and Mythos across a wide range of benchmarks. Key areas of comparison include agentic coding, arcane knowledge, pattern recognition, cyber security tasks, hallucination rates, business simulation, and healthcare. The speaker also touches on OpenAI's stance on recursive self-improvement and discusses model biases and safety implications.

videos.summary: Yannic Kilcher discusses the current state and future of the AI field in response to chat questions during a Minecraft live stream. He explores the theoretical limits of recursive self-improvement, drawing parallels with challenges in reinforcement learning like exploration problems. He also highlights practical applications of current AI technology, specifically mentioning coding assistants and image generation, and notes the shift in research focus towards product development, such as improving agent memory.

videos.summary: This video demonstrates the new Chrome extension for OpenAI's Cortex, which allows the AI assistant to directly interact with the user's browser on macOS and Windows. The extension enables Cortex to work across multiple tabs in parallel, leverage real browser sessions (including cookies and logged-in apps), and execute code to automate complex tasks like research, expense report management, and even spinning up individual "sub agents" to collaborate on tasks in separate browser tabs, all while operating in the background.
videos.title: Codex can now use Chrome directly on macOS and Windows.
videos.transcript_text: Cortex can now help with almost everything. But only if it can work where actual work happens. And for many of us, that place is Chrome. To help with that, we just launched the Chrome extension for Cortex. It works with the Cortex app in both Windows and Mac OS. Let me show you what you can actually do with it and how it works with plugins and other ways that you can connect Cortex to the tools that you already use. If there's a plugin for the app that I need, I normally start there. Connectors are structured and fast and Cortex does not have to click through all the UI just to read a doc or check a message or create a file. Sometimes, however, there is no plugin or there is one, but the thing you need is only available in the full web app. And sometimes the context is actually the existing logged-in Chrome session. This is what the Chrome extension is for. It lets Cortex work in your real browser. Same profile, same session, same cookies, same tabs, same logged-in apps. While the in-app browser is great for local development tasks and giving detailed feedback to Cortex using annotations, the Chrome extension can work across multiple tabs in parallel and use many of the features of the real browser. For example, I can have Cortex do some research on the user sentiment of our latest launches, identify new use cases, highlight pain points, and then take all of that and output it into an actual spreadsheet. Compared to regular computer use, Cortex also won't take over my whole browser. It creates its own Chrome tab group, will work from there, and it can open multiple tabs, scroll through pages, and find content, and then reason along the way to see if it has the correct course. All in the background, so I can keep working undisturbed in my own tabs. Cortex can also combine it with other plugins. For example, to handle my expense reports, it can check my emails using a plugin, search for emails related to my latest trip, extract all the relevant information, and then fill out the expense forms. It can even upload the missing receipts directly from my computer. And because the extension can leverage code execution, Codex can control Chrome without the usual screenshot, reason, move the mouse loop. It can script repetitive work and even work in multiple tabs in parallel. For example, I've been working on this multiplayer drawing game and I can have Codex spin up individual sub agents that play the game together. In this case, each agent has their own browser tab to play in so they can play in parallel and collaborate on the game. The Chrome extension lets you do more by connecting Codex to the tools and apps that you're already using. Give it a try.
